summary: Gruber traces Microsoft's arc from Bill Gates's visionary 1977 goal of 'a computer on every desk and in every home' through its total dominance of the PC era to its failure to adapt when computing moved to mobile. He argues Microsoft achieved its founding vision so completely that it had no follow-up goal, and Steve Ballmer's inability to accept the post-PC world led to missteps like Windows 8. Gruber contrasts Microsoft's dismissal of the iPhone with Google's pragmatic fast-follower approach via Android. He sees new CEO Satya Nadella's background in Microsoft's server/Azure division as a promising sign, since that group already operates in the post-iOS, post-Android reality. Gruber proposes Microsoft's new mission should be cloud services that talk to every networked device, rather than trying to run on every device.
key_points: Gates's 1977 vision was so perfectly achieved that Microsoft became directionless after winning — 'like the dog that caught the car'
Ballmer's fatal error wasn't just laughing at the iPhone, but failing to see what the iPhone would become — a pocket computer that would reshape the industry
Microsoft's 'institutional lack of taste' cost them mobile; Google succeeded as the fast follower that Microsoft's DNA should have enabled
Windows 8 was designed to restore the old world order rather than adapt to the new one, displeasing existing users while failing to gain tablet traction
Nadella's Azure group represents the one part of Microsoft built for the post-mobile reality, and cloud computing is the nascent frontier where Microsoft can find its next mission
money_quotes: Once that goal was achieved, I don't think they knew where to go. They were like the dog that caught the car.
What should have scared Microsoft wasn't what the iPhone was in 2007, it was what the iPhone clearly was going to be in 2008, 2009, 2010.
Microsoft's institutional lack of taste had finally come to bite them in their ass.
Google never laughed at the iPhone; it made money from it by providing web search and maps.
Windows 8's design wasn't what was best for any particular device, but instead what seemed best for Ballmer's 'Windows everywhere' vision of the industry and Microsoft's rightful place atop it.
Windows 8 wasn't designed to adjust to the new world; it was designed to turn back the clock to the old one.
The next ubiquity isn't running on every device, it's talking to every device.
In 30 years we'll look back at our networked infrastructure of today and laugh, wondering how we got a damn thing done.
key_insight: Microsoft's founding vision was so thoroughly achieved that the company became rudderless, and its recovery depends on finding a new defining mission — cloud services connecting every device — rather than clinging to the old one of Windows running on every device.
